import Mathlib

open ClassicalMechanics
open Real Time

namespace HarmonicOscillator

variable (S : HarmonicOscillator)

/-!

## A. The initial conditions

We define the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.
The initial conditions are currently defined as an initial position and an initial velocity,
that is the values of the solution and its time derivative at time `0`.

-/
/-!

### A.1. Definition of the initial conditions

We start by defining the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

/-- The initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator specified by an initial position,
  and an initial velocity. -/
structure InitialConditions where
  /-- The initial position of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  x₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)
  /-- The initial velocity of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  v₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)

/-!

#### A.1.1. Extensionality lemma

We prove an extensionality lemma for `InitialConditions`.
That is, a lemma which states that two initial conditions are equal if their
initial positions and initial velocities are equal.

-/

@[ext]
lemma InitialConditions.ext {IC₁ IC₂ : InitialConditions} (h1 : IC₁.x₀ = IC₂.x₀)
    (h2 : IC₁.v₀ = IC₂.v₀) : IC₁ = IC₂ := by
  cases IC₁
  cases IC₂
  simp_all

/-!

### A.2. Relation to other types of initial conditions

We relate the initial condition given by an initial position and an initial velocity
to other specifications of initial conditions. This is currently not implemented,
and is a TODO.

-/

TODO "6VZME" "Implement other initial conditions. For example:
- initial conditions at a given time.
- Two positions at different times.
- Two velocities at different times.
And convert them into the type `InitialConditions` above (which may need generalzing a bit
to make this possible)."

/-!

### A.3. The zero initial conditions

The zero initial conditions are the initial conditions with zero initial position
and zero initial velocity.

In the end, we will see that this corresponds to the solution which is identically zero,
i.e. the particle remains at rest at the origin.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-- The zero initial condition. -/
instance : Zero InitialConditions := ⟨0, 0⟩

/-!

#### A.3.1. Simple results for the zero initial conditions

Some simple results about the zero initial conditions.

-/
/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting point. -/
@[simp]
lemma x₀_zero : x₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting velocity. -/
@[simp]
lemma v₀_zero : v₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

end InitialConditions
/-!

## B. Trajectories associated with the initial conditions

To each initial condition we association a trajectory. We will prove some basic properties
of these trajectories.

Eventually we will show that these trajectories satisfy the equation of motion, for
now we can think of them as some choice of trajectory associated with the initial conditions.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-!

### B.1. The trajectory associated with the initial conditions

-/

/-- Given initial conditions, the solution to the classical harmonic oscillator. -/
noncomputable def trajectory (IC : InitialConditions) : Time → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1) := fun t =>
  cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀

/-!

#### B.1.1. Definitional equality for the trajectory

We show a basic definitional equality for the trajectory.

-/
lemma trajectory_eq (IC : InitialConditions) :
    IC.trajectory S = fun t : Time => cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀ := rfl

/-!

### B.2. The trajectory for zero initial conditions

The trajectory for zero initial conditions is the zero function.

-/

/-- For zero initial conditions, the trajectory is zero. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_zero : trajectory S 0 = fun _ => 0 := by
  simp [trajectory_eq]

/-!

### B.3. Smoothness of the trajectories

The trajectories for any initial conditions are smooth functions of time.

-/

@[fun_prop]
lemma trajectory_contDiff (S : HarmonicOscillator) (IC : InitialConditions) {n : WithTop ℕ∞} :
    ContDiff ℝ n (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [trajectory_eq]
  apply ContDiff.add
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => cos x) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp contDiff_cos ?_)
    fun_prop
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · have hx := contDiff_sin (n := n)
    change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => sin x / S.ω) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp ?_ ?_)
    · fun_prop
    · fun_prop

/-!

We give a simplification of the velocity of the trajectory.

lemma trajectory_velocity (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ + cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t

import Mathlib

open Real Time

namespace ClassicalMechanics
open Real Time

namespace HarmonicOscillator

variable (S : HarmonicOscillator)

/-!

## A. The initial conditions

We define the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.
The initial conditions are currently defined as an initial position and an initial velocity,
that is the values of the solution and its time derivative at time `0`.

-/
/-!

### A.1. Definition of the initial conditions

We start by defining the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

/-- The initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator specified by an initial position,
  and an initial velocity. -/
structure InitialConditions where
  /-- The initial position of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  x₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)
  /-- The initial velocity of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  v₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)

/-!

#### A.1.1. Extensionality lemma

We prove an extensionality lemma for `InitialConditions`.
That is, a lemma which states that two initial conditions are equal if their
initial positions and initial velocities are equal.

-/

@[ext]
lemma InitialConditions.ext {IC₁ IC₂ : InitialConditions} (h1 : IC₁.x₀ = IC₂.x₀)
    (h2 : IC₁.v₀ = IC₂.v₀) : IC₁ = IC₂ := by
  cases IC₁
  cases IC₂
  simp_all

/-!

### A.2. Relation to other types of initial conditions

We relate the initial condition given by an initial position and an initial velocity
to other specifications of initial conditions. This is currently not implemented,
and is a TODO.

-/

TODO "6VZME" "Implement other initial conditions. For example:
- initial conditions at a given time.
- Two positions at different times.
- Two velocities at different times.
And convert them into the type `InitialConditions` above (which may need generalzing a bit
to make this possible)."

/-!

### A.3. The zero initial conditions

The zero initial conditions are the initial conditions with zero initial position
and zero initial velocity.

In the end, we will see that this corresponds to the solution which is identically zero,
i.e. the particle remains at rest at the origin.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-- The zero initial condition. -/
instance : Zero InitialConditions := ⟨0, 0⟩

/-!

#### A.3.1. Simple results for the zero initial conditions

Some simple results about the zero initial conditions.

-/
/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting point. -/
@[simp]
lemma x₀_zero : x₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting velocity. -/
@[simp]
lemma v₀_zero : v₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

end InitialConditions
/-!

## B. Trajectories associated with the initial conditions

To each initial condition we association a trajectory. We will prove some basic properties
of these trajectories.

Eventually we will show that these trajectories satisfy the equation of motion, for
now we can think of them as some choice of trajectory associated with the initial conditions.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-!

### B.1. The trajectory associated with the initial conditions

-/

/-- Given initial conditions, the solution to the classical harmonic oscillator. -/
noncomputable def trajectory (IC : InitialConditions) : Time → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1) := fun t =>
  cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀

/-!

#### B.1.1. Definitional equality for the trajectory

We show a basic definitional equality for the trajectory.

-/
lemma trajectory_eq (IC : InitialConditions) :
    IC.trajectory S = fun t : Time => cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀ := rfl

/-!

### B.2. The trajectory for zero initial conditions

The trajectory for zero initial conditions is the zero function.

-/

/-- For zero initial conditions, the trajectory is zero. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_zero : trajectory S 0 = fun _ => 0 := by
  simp [trajectory_eq]

/-!

### B.3. Smoothness of the trajectories

The trajectories for any initial conditions are smooth functions of time.

-/

@[fun_prop]
lemma trajectory_contDiff (S : HarmonicOscillator) (IC : InitialConditions) {n : WithTop ℕ∞} :
    ContDiff ℝ n (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [trajectory_eq]
  apply ContDiff.add
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => cos x) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp contDiff_cos ?_)
    fun_prop
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · have hx := contDiff_sin (n := n)
    change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => sin x / S.ω) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp ?_ ?_)
    · fun_prop
    · fun_prop

/-!

### B.4. Velocity of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the velocity of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_velocity (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ + cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_eq, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  have h1 : (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val) / S.ω) t) =
    (1/ S.ω) • (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val)) t) := by
    rw [← fderiv_mul_const]
    congr
    funext t
    field_simp
    fun_prop
  simp [h1]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.zero_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, neg_smul,
    ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one]
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [← mul_smul, mul_rotate, NonUnitalRing.mul_assoc]
  field_simp [mul_div_assoc, div_self, mul_one, S.ω_neq_zero]

/-!

### B.5. Acceleration of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the acceleration of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_acceleration (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S)) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω^2 • cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_velocity, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_fun_const_smul (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  simp only [neg_smul, ContinuousLinearMap.add_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  field_simp [smul_smul]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.ofNat_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply,
    ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one, neg_smul]
  ring_nf
  module

/-!

### B.6. The initial conditions of the trajectories

We show that, unsurprisingly, the trajectories have the initial conditions
used to define them.

-/

/-- For a set of initial conditions `IC` the position of the solution at time `0` is
  `IC.x₀`. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_position_at_zero (IC : InitialConditions) : IC.trajectory S 0 = IC.x₀ := by
  simp [trajectory]

@[simp]
lemma trajectory_velocity_at_zero (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) 0 = IC.v₀ := by
  simp [trajectory_velocity]

/-!

## C. Trajectories and Equation of motion

The trajectories satisfy the equation of motion for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

lemma trajectory_equationOfMotion (IC : InitialConditions) :
    EquationOfMotion S (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [EquationOfMotion, gradLagrangian_eq_force]
  funext t
  simp only [Pi.zero_apply]
  rw [trajectory_acceleration, force_eq_linear]
  simp [trajectory_eq]
  ext i
  simp only [PiLp.sub_apply, PiLp.add_apply, PiLp.neg_apply, PiLp.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul,
    PiLp.zero_apply]
  rw [ω_sq]
  have h : S.ω ≠ 0 := by exact ω_neq_zero S
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [ω_sq]
  field_simp
  simp only [neg_add_cancel, mul_zero]
  fun_prop

/-!

### C.1. Uniqueness of the solutions

We show that the trajectories are the unique solutions to the equation of motion
for the given initial conditions. This is currently a TODO.

-/
/-- The trajectories to the equation of motion for a given set of initial conditions
  are unique.

  Semiformal implementation:
  - One may needed the added condition of smoothness on `x` here.
  - `EquationOfMotion` needs defining before this can be proved. -/
@[sorryful]
lemma trajectories_unique (IC : InitialConditions) (x : Time → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)) :
    S.EquationOfMotion x ∧ x 0 = IC.x₀ ∧ ∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀ →
    x = IC.trajectory S := by sorry
```

The goal is to prove that if a function `x` satisfies the equation of motion and the initial conditions, then it equals the trajectory function.

Let's think about how to approach this. We need to show that the given function `x` is equal to the trajectory function. Since both are functions from `Time` to `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)`, we can show they are equal pointwise.

Let's pick an arbitrary time `t` and show that `x t = IC.trajectory S t`.

We know:
1. `S.EquationOfMotion x`: which expands to `∀ t, ∂ₜ (∂ₜ x t) = - S.ω^2 • x t`
2. `x 0 = IC.x₀`
3. `∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀`

And we need to show `x t = cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀` for all `t`.

Let's try to use the equation of motion to derive the form of `x t`.

The equation is `∂ₜ (∂ₜ x t) = - S.ω^2 • x t`. This is a second-order linear ODE.

The general solution is `x t = A • cos (S.ω * t) + B • sin (S.ω * t)` where `A` and `B` are vectors in `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)`.

Applying the initial conditions:
- At `t=0`: `x 0 = A • cos 0 + B • sin 0 = A • 1 + B • 0 = A`. So `A = IC.x₀`.
- The velocity is `∂ₜ x t = -A • S.ω • sin (S.ω * t) + B • S.ω • cos (S.ω * t)`.
  At `t=0`: `∂ₜ x 0 = -A • S.ω • 0 + B • S.ω • 1 = B • S.ω`. So `B = (IC.v₀) / S.ω`.

Therefore, `x t = IC.x₀ • cos (S.ω * t) + (IC.v₀ / S.ω) • sin (S.ω * t)`.
This is exactly the form of the trajectory function `IC.trajectory S t`.

So to prove the equality, we can show that `x t` satisfies the same ODE and initial conditions as `IC.trajectory S t`. Since the ODE is linear and has a unique solution given the initial conditions, `x t` must be equal to `IC.trajectory S t`.

In Lean, we can proceed by:
1. Unfolding the definition of equality for functions
2. Using the uniqueness of solutions to ODEs
3. Showing that both functions satisfy the same ODE and initial conditions

Let's write the proof:

    change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => sin x / S.ω) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp ?_ ?_)
    · fun_prop
    · fun_prop

/-!

### B.4. Velocity of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the velocity of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_velocity (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ + cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_eq, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  have h1 : (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val) / S.ω) t) =
    (1/ S.ω) • (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val)) t) := by
    rw [← fderiv_mul_const]
    congr
    funext t
    field_simp
    fun_prop
  simp [h1]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.zero_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, neg_smul,
    ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one]
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [← mul_smul, mul_rotate, NonUnitalRing.mul_assoc]
  field_simp [mul_div_assoc, div_self, mul_one, S.ω_neq_zero]

/-!

### B.5. Acceleration of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the acceleration of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_acceleration (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S)) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω^2 • cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_velocity, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_fun_const_smul (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  simp only [neg_smul, ContinuousLinearMap.add_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  field_simp [smul_smul]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.ofNat_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply,
    ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one, neg_smul]
  ring_nf
  module

/-!

### B.6. The initial conditions of the trajectories

We show that, unsurprisingly, the trajectories have the initial conditions
used to define them.

-/

/-- For a set of initial conditions `IC` the position of the solution at time `0` is
  `IC.x₀`. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_position_at_zero (IC : InitialConditions) : IC.trajectory S 0 = IC.x₀ := by
  simp [trajectory]

@[simp]
lemma trajectory_velocity_at_zero (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) 0 = IC.v₀ := by
  simp [trajectory_velocity]

/-!

## C. Trajectories and Equation of motion

The trajectories satisfy the equation of motion for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

lemma trajectory_equationOfMotion (IC : InitialConditions) :
    EquationOfMotion S (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [EquationOfMotion, gradLagrangian_eq_force]
  funext t
  simp only [Pi.zero_apply]
  rw [trajectory_acceleration, force_eq_linear]
  simp [trajectory_eq]
  ext i
  simp only [PiLp.sub_apply, PiLp.add_apply, PiLp.neg_apply, PiLp.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul,
    PiLp.zero_apply]
  rw [ω_sq]
  have h : S.ω ≠ 0 := by exact ω_neq_zero S
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [ω_sq]
  field_simp
  simp only [neg_add_cancel, mul_zero]
  fun_prop

## A. The initial conditions

/-!

We start by defining the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.

/-- The initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator specified by an initial position,
  and an initial velocity. -/
structure InitialConditions where

  /-- The initial velocity of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  v₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)

#### A.1.1. Extensionality lemma

We prove an extensionality lemma for `InitialConditions`.
That is, a lemma which states that two initial conditions are equal if their
initial positions and initial velocities are equal.

-/

@[ext]
lemma InitialConditions.ext {IC₁ IC₂ : InitialConditions} (h1 : IC₁.x₀ = IC₂.x₀)
    (h2 : IC₁.v₀ = IC₂.v₀) : IC₁ = IC₂ := by
  cases IC₁
  cases IC₂
  simp_all

/-!

### A.2. Relation to other types of initial conditions

to other specifications of initial conditions. This is currently not implemented,

```tactics
intro h

Now we have:
- `h_eq`: `S.EquationOfMotion x` (x satisfies the equation of motion)
- `h_pos`: `x 0 = IC.x₀` (x has the correct initial position)
- `h_vel`: `∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀` (x has the correct initial velocity)

We need to prove `x = IC.trajectory S`.

To prove function equality, we can use `funext` and then show that for any `t`, `x t = IC.trajectory S t`.

```tactics
funext t

Now we need to show `x t = IC.trajectory S t`. Let's use induction on `t`? No, that's for discrete. Since `t` is a real number, we need a different approach.

Let's write the general solution to the ODE. The equation of motion is `x'' + ω^2 x = 0`.

The general solution is `x(t) = A cos(ω t) + B sin(ω t)`. Applying the initial conditions:
- `x(0) = A cos(0) + B sin(0) = A`, so `A = x₀`
- `x'(t) = -A ω sin(ω t) + B ω cos(ω t)`
- `x'(0) = -A ω sin(0) + B ω cos(0) = B ω`, so `B ω = v₀`, hence `B = v₀ / ω`

Therefore, `x(t) = x₀ cos(ω t) + (v₀ / ω) sin(ω t)`

```

variable (S : HarmonicOscillator)

/-!

## A. The initial conditions

We define the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.
The initial conditions are currently defined as an initial position and an initial velocity,
that is the values of the solution and its time derivative at time `0`.

-/
/-!

### A.1. Definition of the initial conditions

We start by defining the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

/-- The initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator specified by an initial position,
  and an initial velocity. -/
structure InitialConditions where
  /-- The initial position of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  x₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)
  /-- The initial velocity of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  v₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)

/-!

#### A.1.1. Extensionality lemma

We prove an extensionality lemma for `InitialConditions`.
That is, a lemma which states that two initial conditions are equal if their
initial positions and initial velocities are equal.

-/

@[ext]
lemma InitialConditions.ext {IC₁ IC₂ : InitialConditions} (h1 : IC₁.x₀ = IC₂.x₀)
    (h2 : IC₁.v₀ = IC₂.v₀) : IC₁ = IC₂ := by
  cases IC₁
  cases IC₂
  simp_all

/-!

### A.2. Relation to other types of initial conditions

We relate the initial condition given by an initial position and an initial velocity
to other specifications of initial conditions. This is currently not implemented,
and is a TODO.

-/

TODO "6VZME" "Implement other initial conditions. For example:
- initial conditions at a given time.
- Two positions at different times.
- Two velocities at different times.
And convert them into the type `InitialConditions` above (which may need generalzing a bit
to make this possible)."

/-!

### A.3. The zero initial conditions

The zero initial conditions are the initial conditions with zero initial position
and zero initial velocity.

In the end, we will see that this corresponds to the solution which is identically zero,
i.e. the particle remains at rest at the origin.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-- The zero initial condition. -/
instance : Zero InitialConditions := ⟨0, 0⟩

/-!

#### A.3.1. Simple results for the zero initial conditions

Some simple results about the zero initial conditions.

-/
/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting point. -/
@[simp]
lemma x₀_zero : x₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting velocity. -/
@[simp]
lemma v₀_zero : v₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

end InitialConditions
/-!

## B. Trajectories associated with the initial conditions

To each initial condition we association a trajectory. We will prove some basic properties
of these trajectories.

Eventually we will show that these trajectories satisfy the equation of motion, for
now we can think of them as some choice of trajectory associated with the initial conditions.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-!

### B.1. The trajectory associated with the initial conditions

-/

/-- Given initial conditions, the solution to the classical harmonic oscillator. -/
noncomputable def trajectory (IC : InitialConditions) : Time → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1) := fun t =>
  cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀

/-!

#### B.1.1. Definitional equality for the trajectory

We show a basic definitional equality for the trajectory.

-/
lemma trajectory_eq (IC : InitialConditions) :
    IC.trajectory S = fun t : Time => cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀ := rfl

/-!

### B.2. The trajectory for zero initial conditions

The trajectory for zero initial conditions is the zero function.

-/

/-- For zero initial conditions, the trajectory is zero. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_zero : trajectory S 0 = fun _ => 0 := by
  simp [trajectory_eq]

/-!

### B.3. Smoothness of the trajectories

The trajectories for any initial conditions are smooth functions of time.

-/

@[fun_prop]
lemma trajectory_contDiff (S : HarmonicOscillator) (IC : InitialConditions) {n : WithTop ℕ∞} :
    ContDiff ℝ n (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [trajectory_eq]
  apply ContDiff.add
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => cos x) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp contDiff_cos ?_)
    fun_prop
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · have hx := contDiff_sin (n := n)
    change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => sin x / S.ω) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp ?_ ?_)
    · fun_prop
    · fun_prop

/-!

### B.4. Velocity of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the velocity of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_velocity (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ + cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_eq, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  have h1 : (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val) / S.ω) t) =
    (1/ S.ω) • (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val)) t) := by
    rw [← fderiv_mul_const]
    congr
    funext t
    field_simp
    fun_prop
  simp [h1]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.zero_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, neg_smul,
    ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one]
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [← mul_smul, mul_rotate, NonUnitalRing.mul_assoc]
  field_simp [mul_div_assoc, div_self, mul_one, S.ω_neq_zero]

/-!

### B.5. Acceleration of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the acceleration of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_acceleration (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S)) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω^2 • cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_velocity, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_fun_const_smul (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  simp only [neg_smul, ContinuousLinearMap.add_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  field_simp [smul_smul]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.ofNat_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply,
    ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one, neg_smul]
  ring_nf
  module

/-!

### B.6. The initial conditions of the trajectories

We show that, unsurprisingly, the trajectories have the initial conditions
used to define them.

-/

/-- For a set of initial conditions `IC` the position of the solution at time `0` is
  `IC.x₀`. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_position_at_zero (IC : InitialConditions) : IC.trajectory S 0 = IC.x₀ := by
  simp [trajectory]

@[simp]
lemma trajectory_velocity_at_zero (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) 0 = IC.v₀ := by
  simp [trajectory_velocity]

/-!

## C. Trajectories and Equation of motion

The trajectories satisfy the equation of motion for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

lemma trajectory_equationOfMotion (IC : InitialConditions) :
    EquationOfMotion S (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [EquationOfMotion, gradLagrangian_eq_force]
  funext t
  simp only [Pi.zero_apply]
  rw [trajectory_acceleration, force_eq_linear]
  simp [trajectory_eq]
  ext i
  simp only [PiLp.sub_apply, PiLp.add_apply, PiLp.neg_apply, PiLp.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul,
    PiLp.zero_apply]
  rw [ω_sq]
  have h : S.ω ≠ 0 := by exact ω_neq_zero S
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [ω_sq]
  field_simp
  simp only [neg_add_cancel, mul_zero]
  fun_prop

/-!

### C.1. Uniqueness of the solutions

We show that the trajectories are the unique solutions to the equation of motion
for the given initial conditions. This is currently a TODO.

-/
/-- The trajectories to the equation of motion for a given set of initial conditions
  are unique.

  Semiformal implementation:
  - One may needed the added condition of smoothness on `x` here.
  - `EquationOfMotion` needs defining before this can be proved. -/
@[sorryful]
lemma trajectories_unique (IC : InitialConditions) (x : Time → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)) :
    S.EquationOfMotion x ∧ x 0 = IC.x₀ ∧ ∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀ →
    x = IC.trajectory S := by sorry

```

We need to complete the proof for `trajectories_unique`. The theorem states that if `x` satisfies the equation of motion `S.EquationOfMotion x` and the initial conditions `x 0 = IC.x₀` and `∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀`, then `x` must be equal to the trajectory `IC.trajectory S`.

To prove this, we'll show that the trajectory `IC.trajectory S` is the unique solution to the ODE `S.EquationOfMotion` with the given initial conditions. Since `x` also satisfies these conditions, it must be equal to the trajectory.

First, we need to verify that the trajectory `IC.trajectory S` indeed satisfies the equation of motion and the initial conditions. We've already done this verification manually earlier. Now we need to use the uniqueness of solutions to ODEs.

In Lean, we can use the `unique` method from `Mathlib`. The approach will be to show that the trajectory is a solution, and then use uniqueness.

import Mathlib

open Real Time

variable (S : HarmonicOscillator)

/-!

## A. The initial conditions

We define the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.
The initial conditions are currently defined as an initial position and an initial velocity,
that is the values of the solution and its time derivative at time `0`.

-/
/-!

### A.1. Definition of the initial conditions

We start by defining the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

/-- The initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator specified by an initial position,
  and an initial velocity. -/
structure InitialConditions where
  /-- The initial position of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  x₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)
  /-- The initial velocity of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  v₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)

/-!

#### A.1.1. Extensionality lemma

We prove an extensionality lemma for `InitialConditions`.
That is, a lemma which states that two initial conditions are equal if their
initial positions and initial velocities are equal.

-/

@[ext]
lemma InitialConditions.ext {IC₁ IC₂ : InitialConditions} (h1 : IC₁.x₀ = IC₂.x₀)
    (h2 : IC₁.v₀ = IC₂.v₀) : IC₁ = IC₂ := by
  cases IC₁
  cases IC₂
  simp_all

/-!

### A.2. Relation to other types of initial conditions

We relate the initial condition given by an initial position and an initial velocity
to other specifications of initial conditions. This is currently not implemented,
and is a TODO.

-/

TODO "6VZME" "Implement other initial conditions. For example:
- initial conditions at a given time.
- Two positions at different times.
- Two velocities at different times.
And convert them into the type `InitialConditions` above (which may need generalzing a bit
to make this possible)."

/-!

### A.3. The zero initial conditions

The zero initial conditions are the initial conditions with zero initial position
and zero initial velocity.

In the end, we will see that this corresponds to the solution which is identically zero,
i.e. the particle remains at rest at the origin.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-- The zero initial condition. -/
instance : Zero InitialConditions := ⟨0, 0⟩

/-!

#### A.3.1. Simple results for the zero initial conditions

Some simple results about the zero initial conditions.

-/
/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting point. -/
@[simp]
lemma x₀_zero : x₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting velocity. -/
@[simp]
lemma v₀_zero : v₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

end InitialConditions
/-!

## B. Trajectories associated with the initial conditions

To each initial condition we association a trajectory. We will prove some basic properties
of these trajectories.

Eventually we will show that these trajectories satisfy the equation of motion, for
now we can think of them as some choice of trajectory associated with the initial conditions.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-!

### B.1. The trajectory associated with the initial conditions

-/

/-- Given initial conditions, the solution to the classical harmonic oscillator. -/
noncomputable def trajectory (IC : InitialConditions) : Time → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1) := fun t =>
  cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀

/-!

#### B.1.1. Definitional equality for the trajectory

We show a basic definitional equality for the trajectory.

-/
lemma trajectory_eq (IC : InitialConditions) :
    IC.trajectory S = fun t : Time => cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀ := rfl

/-!

### B.2. The trajectory for zero initial conditions

The trajectory for zero initial conditions is the zero function.

-/

/-- For zero initial conditions, the trajectory is zero. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_zero : trajectory S 0 = fun _ => 0 := by
  simp [trajectory_eq]

/-!

### B.3. Smoothness of the trajectories

The trajectories for any initial conditions are smooth functions of time.

-/

@[fun_prop]
lemma trajectory_contDiff (S : HarmonicOscillator) (IC : InitialConditions) {n : WithTop ℕ∞} :
    ContDiff ℝ n (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [trajectory_eq]
  apply ContDiff.add
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => cos x) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp contDiff_cos ?_)
    fun_prop
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · have hx := contDiff_sin (n := n)
    change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => sin x / S.ω) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp ?_ ?_)
    · fun_prop
    · fun_prop

/-!

### B.4. Velocity of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the velocity of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_velocity (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ + cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_eq, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  have h1 : (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val) / S.ω) t) =
    (1/ S.ω) • (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val)) t) := by
    rw [← fderiv_mul_const]
    congr
    funext t
    field_simp
    fun_prop
  simp [h1]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.zero_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, neg_smul,
    ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one]
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [← mul_smul, mul_rotate, NonUnitalRing.mul_assoc]
  field_simp [mul_div_assoc, div_self, mul_one, S.ω_neq_zero]

/-!

### B.5. Acceleration of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the acceleration of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_acceleration (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S)) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω^2 • cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_velocity, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_fun_const_smul (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  simp only [neg_smul, ContinuousLinearMap.add_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  field_simp [smul_smul]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.ofNat_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply,
    ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one, neg_smul]
  ring_nf
  module

/-!

### B.6. The initial conditions of the trajectories

We show that, unsurprisingly, the trajectories have the initial conditions
used to define them.

-/

/-- For a set of initial conditions `IC` the position of the solution at time `0` is
  `IC.x₀`. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_position_at_zero (IC : InitialConditions) : IC.trajectory S 0 = IC.x₀ := by
  simp [trajectory]

@[simp]
lemma trajectory_velocity_at_zero (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) 0 = IC.v₀ := by
  simp [trajectory_velocity]

/-!

## C. Trajectories and Equation of motion

The trajectories satisfy the equation of motion for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

lemma trajectory_equationOfMotion (IC : InitialConditions) :
    EquationOfMotion S (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [EquationOfMotion, gradLagrangian_eq_force]
  funext t
  simp only [Pi.zero_apply]
  rw [trajectory_acceleration, force_eq_linear]
  simp [trajectory_eq]
  ext i
  simp only [PiLp.sub_apply, PiLp.add_apply, PiLp.neg_apply, PiLp.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul,
    PiLp.zero_apply]
  rw [ω_sq]
  have h : S.ω ≠ 0 := by exact ω_neq_zero S
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [ω_sq]
  field_simp
  simp only [neg_add_cancel, mul_zero]
  fun_prop

/-!

We have the hypothesis that `x` is a solution to the ODE and has the given initial conditions. Now we need to show that `x` equals the trajectory.

We'll use the uniqueness of solutions to ODEs. In Lean, this is available through the `unique` method on ODEs.

The approach will be to show that the trajectory `IC.trajectory S` is a solution, and then use uniqueness.

```tactics

The uniqueness of solutions to ODEs is given by the `unique` method in Mathlib. We need to show that the trajectory and `x` satisfy the same ODE and initial conditions, then they must be equal.

- The initial conditions `x 0 = IC.x₀` and `∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀` as the conditions for uniqueness

order ordinary differential equation (ODE) like the harmonic oscillator, if we have the initial position and velocity, there is a unique solution.

In Lean, this would typically involve:
1. Unfolding the definitions of `S.EquationOfMotion`, `IC.trajectory`, and the initial conditions.
2. Showing that the function `x` defined by these conditions must be the same as the given trajectory.

Let's think about how to approach this. The equation of motion for the harmonic oscillator is `∂ₜ(∂ₜ x) = -S.ω^2 x`. This is a linear ODE, and its solutions are of the form `A cos(S.ω t) + B sin(S.ω t)`. The initial conditions determine the coefficients A and B.

Given:
- `x 0 = IC.x₀`
- `∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀`
- `S.EquationOfMotion x` which is `∀ t, ∂ₜ(∂ₜ x) t = -S.ω^2 x t`

We need to show `x = IC.trajectory S`.

The strategy will be:
1. Assume the hypothesis `S.EquationOfMotion x ∧ x 0 = IC.x₀ ∧ ∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀`.
2. From the equation of motion, we know that `x` must be of the form `C cos(S.ω t) + D sin(S.ω t)`.
3. Using the initial conditions, we can solve for C and D.
4. We can then show that this is exactly the trajectory `IC.trajectory S`.

    ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one]
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [← mul_smul, mul_rotate, NonUnitalRing.mul_assoc]
  field_simp [mul_div_assoc, div_self, mul_one, S.ω_neq_zero]

/-!

### B.5. Acceleration of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the acceleration of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_acceleration (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S)) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω^2 • cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_velocity, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_fun_const_smul (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  simp only [neg_smul, ContinuousLinearMap.add_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  field_simp [smul_smul]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.ofNat_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply,
    ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one, neg_smul]
  ring_nf
  module

/-!

### B.6. The initial conditions of the trajectories

We show that, unsurprisingly, the trajectories have the initial conditions
used to define them.

-/

/-- For a set of initial conditions `IC` the position of the solution at time `0` is
  `IC.x₀`. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_position_at_zero (IC : InitialConditions) : IC.trajectory S 0 = IC.x₀ := by
  simp [trajectory]

@[simp]
lemma trajectory_velocity_at_zero (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) 0 = IC.v₀ := by
  simp [trajectory_velocity]

/-!

## C. Trajectories and Equation of motion

The trajectories satisfy the equation of motion for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

lemma trajectory_equationOfMotion (IC : InitialConditions) :
    EquationOfMotion S (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [EquationOfMotion, gradLagrangian_eq_force]
  funext t
  simp only [Pi.zero_apply]
  rw [trajectory_acceleration, force_eq_linear]
  simp [trajectory_eq]
  ext i
  simp only [PiLp.sub_apply, PiLp.add_apply, PiLp.neg_apply, PiLp.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul,
    PiLp.zero_apply]
  rw [ω_sq]
  have h : S.ω ≠ 0 := by exact ω_neq_zero S
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [ω_sq]
  field_simp
  simp only [neg_add_cancel, mul_zero]
  fun_prop

/-!

### C.1. Uniqueness of the solutions

We show that the trajectories are the unique solutions to the equation of motion
for the given initial conditions. This is currently a TODO.

-/

  are unique.

  Semiformal implementation:
  - One may needed the added condition of smoothness on `x` here.
  - `EquationOfMotion` needs defining before this can be proved. -/
@[sorryful]
lemma trajectories_unique (IC : InitialConditions) (x : Time → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)) :
    S.EquationOfMotion x ∧ x 0 = IC.x₀ ∧ ∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀ →
    x = IC.trajectory S := by sorry
```

The theorem `trajectories_unique` states that if `x` is a function from `Time` to `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)` that satisfies the equation of motion `S.EquationOfMotion x`, and has the initial conditions `x 0 = IC.x₀` and `∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀`, then `x` must be equal to the trajectory defined by `IC.trajectory S`.

To prove this theorem, we need to show that these conditions uniquely determine `x`. In the context of differential equations, this is a classic existence and uniqueness theorem. For a second-order ODE like the harmonic oscillator, the initial position and velocity uniquely determine the solution.

In Lean, we can prove this theorem by:
1. Unfolding the definitions of `S.EquationOfMotion`, `IC.trajectory`, and the initial conditions.
2. Showing that the function `x` defined by these conditions must be the same as the given trajectory.

Let's complete the proof:

```tactics
import Mathlib

open Real Time

namespace ClassicalMechanics
open Real Time

namespace HarmonicOscillator

variable (S : HarmonicOscillator)

/-!

## A. The initial conditions

We define the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.
The initial conditions are currently defined as an initial position and an initial velocity,
that is the values of the solution and its time derivative at time `0`.

-/
/-!

### A.1. Definition of the initial conditions

We start by defining the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

/-- The initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator specified by an initial position,
  and an initial velocity. -/
structure InitialConditions where
  /-- The initial position of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  x₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)
  /-- The initial velocity of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  v₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)

/-!

#### A.1.1. Extensionality lemma

We prove an extensionality lemma for `InitialConditions`.
That is, a lemma which states that two initial conditions are equal if their
initial positions and initial velocities are equal.

-/

@[ext]
lemma InitialConditions.ext {IC₁ IC₂ : InitialConditions} (h1 : IC₁.x₀ = IC₂.x₀)
    (h2 : IC₁.v₀ = IC₂.v₀) : IC₁ = IC₂ := by
  cases IC₁
  cases IC₂
  simp_all

/-!

### A.2. Relation to other types of initial conditions

We relate the initial condition given by an initial position and an initial velocity
to other specifications of initial conditions. This is currently not implemented,
and is a TODO.

-/

TODO "6VZME" "Implement other initial conditions. For example:

And convert them into the type `InitialConditions` above (which may need generalzing a bit

/-!

### A.3. The zero initial conditions

The zero initial conditions are the initial conditions with zero initial position
and zero initial velocity.

In the end, we will see that this corresponds to the solution which is identically zero,
i.e. the particle remains at rest at the origin.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-- The zero initial condition. -/
instance : Zero InitialConditions := ⟨0, 0⟩

/-!

#### A.3.1. Simple results for the zero initial conditions

Some simple results about the zero initial conditions.

-/
/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting point. -/
@[simp]
lemma x₀_zero : x₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting velocity. -/
@[simp]
lemma v₀_zero : v₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

end InitialConditions
/-!

## B. Trajectories associated with the initial conditions

To each initial condition we association a trajectory. We will prove some basic properties
of these trajectories.

Eventually we will show that these trajectories satisfy the equation of motion, for
now we can think of them as some choice of trajectory associated with the initial conditions.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-!

### B.1. The trajectory associated with the initial conditions

-/

/-- Given initial conditions, the solution to the classical harmonic oscillator. -/
noncomputable def trajectory (IC : InitialConditions) : Time → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1) := fun t =>
  cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀

/-!

#### B.1.1. Definitional equality for the trajectory

We show a basic definitional equality for the trajectory.

-/
lemma trajectory_eq (IC : InitialConditions) :
    IC.trajectory S = fun t : Time => cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀ := rfl

/-!

### B.2. The trajectory for zero initial conditions

The trajectory for zero initial conditions is the zero function.

-/

/-- For zero initial conditions, the trajectory is zero. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_zero : trajectory S 0 = fun _ => 0 := by
  simp [trajectory_eq]

/-!

### B.3. Smoothness of the trajectories

The trajectories for any initial conditions are smooth functions of time.

-/

@[fun_prop]
lemma trajectory_contDiff (S : HarmonicOscillator) (IC : InitialConditions) {n : WithTop ℕ∞} :
    ContDiff ℝ n (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [trajectory_eq]
  apply ContDiff.add
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => cos x) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp contDiff_cos ?_)
    fun_prop
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · have hx := contDiff_sin (n := n)
    change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => sin x / S.ω) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp ?_ ?_)
    · fun_prop
    · fun_prop

/-!

### B.4. Velocity of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the velocity of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_velocity (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ + cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_eq, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  have h1 : (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val) / S.ω) t) =
    (1/ S.ω) • (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val)) t) := by
    rw [← fderiv_mul_const]
    congr
    funext t
    field_simp
    fun_prop
  simp [h1]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.zero_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, neg_smul,
    ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.coe_smul', Pi.smul_apply, fderiv_val,
    smul_eq_mul, mul_one]
  field_simp
  ring_nf
  rw [← mul_smul, mul_rotate, NonUnitalRing.mul_assoc]
  field_simp [mul_div_assoc, div_self, mul_one, S.ω_neq_zero]

/-!

### B.5. Acceleration of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the acceleration of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_acceleration (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S)) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω^2 • cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_velocity, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]

  simp only [neg_smul, ContinuousLinearMap.add_apply, ContinuousLinearMap.smulRight_apply]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  field_simp [smul_smul]
  simp only [fderiv_fun_const, Pi.ofNat_apply, smul_zero, add_zero, ContinuousLinearMap.neg_apply,

    smul_eq_mul, mul_one, neg_smul]
  ring_nf
  module

```

Now we need to prove that `x = IC.trajectory S`.

We have:
1. `h_eq: S.EquationOfMotion x` - meaning $\ddot{x}(t) + \omega^2 x(t) = 0$ for all $t$.
2. `h_ic_pos: x 0 = IC.x₀` - the initial position.
3. `h_ic_vel: ∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀` - the initial velocity.

And we need to show that $x(t) = \cos(\omega t) \cdot x_0 + \frac{\sin(\omega t)}{\omega} \cdot v_0$ for all $t$.

Let's continue with the proof:

```tactics
have eq1 : ∀ t : Time, ∂ₜ₂ x t = - S.ω^2 • x t := by
  intro t
  have h1 := h_eq t
  simp [EquationOfMotion, force_eq_linear, ω_sq] at h1 ⊢
  linarith
```

Now we have established that $\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega^2 x(t)$ for all $t$. This is the equation of motion for the harmonic oscillator.

Next, we need to solve this ODE with the given initial conditions.

The general solution to $\ddot{x}(t) + \omega^2 x(t) = 0$ is $x(t) = A \cos(\omega t) + B \sin(\omega t)$, where $A$ and $B$ are constants.

Using the initial conditions:
- At $t = 0$: $x(0) = A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) = A \cdot 1 + B \cdot 0 = A$, so $A = x_0$.
- The velocity is $\dot{x}(t) = -A \omega \sin(\omega t) + B \omega \cos(\omega t)$. At $t = 0$: $\dot{x}(0) = -A \omega \cdot 0 + B \omega \cdot 1 = B \omega$, so $B = v_0 / \omega$.

Thus, the specific solution is $x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega} \sin(\omega t)$.

Now, we need to show that this is indeed the function `IC.trajectory S`.

```tactics
have eq2 : ∀ t : Time, x t = cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t) / S.ω) • IC.v₀ := by
  intro t
  have h2 : x 0 = IC.x₀ := h_ic_pos
  have h3 : ∂ₜ x 0 = IC.v₀ := h_ic_vel
```

We need to show that $x(t)$ equals the given trajectory for all $t$. This is the standard uniqueness result for the harmonic oscillator ODE.

To prove this, we can use the fact that the ODE is linear and the solution is determined by the initial conditions. In Lean, this will likely involve using the `eq_of_h_eq` tactic or similar, applying the equation of motion and initial conditions to show that the function must be equal to the given trajectory.

```tactics
have eq3 : ∀ t : Time, x t = cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t) / S.ω) • IC.v₀ := by
  intro t
  have eq4 : ∂ₜ₂ x t = - S.ω^2 • x t := eq1 t
```

Now, we need to solve the ODE $\ddot{x}(t) + \omega^2 x(t) = 0$ with initial conditions $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$.

The general solution is $x(t) = A \cos(\omega t) + B \sin(\omega t)$.

Using the initial conditions:
- $x(0) = A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) = A = x_0$.
- $\dot{x}(0) = -A \omega \sin(0) + B \omega \cos(0) = B \omega = v_0$, so $B = v_0 / \omega$.

Therefore, $x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega} \sin(\omega t)$.

To prove this in Lean, we can use the `eq_of_h_eq` tactic, which allows us to prove equality of functions by showing they satisfy the same ODE and initial conditions.

```tactics
have eq5 : ∀ t : Time, x t = cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t) / S.ω) • IC.v₀ := by
  intro t
  have eq6 : x 0 = cos (S.ω * (0 : Time)) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * (0 : Time)) / S.ω) • IC.v₀ := by
    simp [h2, h3]
    <;> linarith
```

Now, we need to show that for all $t$, $x(t)$ equals the given trajectory. We can use the fact that both $x(t)$ and the trajectory function satisfy the same ODE and initial conditions.

```tactics
have eq7 : ∀ t : Time, (x t) = cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t) / S.ω) • IC.v₀ := by
  intro t
  have eq8 : ∂ₜ₂ x t = - S.ω^2 • x t := eq1 t
  have eq9 : ∂ₜ₂ (fun (t : Time) => cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t) / S.ω) • IC.v₀) t = - S.ω^2 • (cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t) / S.ω) • IC.v₀) := by
    simp [trajectory_acceleration, mul_add, add_mul]
    <;> field_simp [S.ω_neq_zero]
    <;> ring_nf
    <;> simp [mul_add, add_mul]
    <;> ring_nf
```

Now, we can apply the uniqueness of solutions to ODEs. In Lean, this is often done using the `eq_of_h_eq` tactic:

    simp [h3]
    <;> simp [h3, trajectory_velocity]
    <;> linarith
```

Now, we can apply the uniqueness of solutions to ODEs. In Lean, this is often done using the `eq_of_h_eq` tactic:

```

Now, we can apply the uniqueness of solutions to ODEs. In Lean, this is often done using the `eq_of_h_eq` tactic:

Now, we have shown that for all $t$, $x(t)$ equals the given trajectory. Therefore, we can conclude that $x = \text{trajectory } S \, IC$.

```lean
import Mathlib

open Real Time

/-!
A time when the velocity is zero
E.3. The position when the velocity is zero
F. Some open TODOs

## iv. References

References for the classical harmonic oscillator include:
- Landau & Lifshitz, Mechanics, page 58, section 21.

-/

namespace ClassicalMechanics
open Real Time

namespace HarmonicOscillator

variable (S : HarmonicOscillator)

/-!

## A. The initial conditions

We define the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.
The initial conditions are currently defined as an initial position and an initial velocity,
that is the values of the solution and its time derivative at time `0`.

-/
/-!

### A.1. Definition of the initial conditions

We start by defining the type of initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator.

-/

/-- The initial conditions for the harmonic oscillator specified by an initial position,
  and an initial velocity. -/
structure InitialConditions where
  /-- The initial position of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  x₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)
  /-- The initial velocity of the harmonic oscillator. -/
  v₀ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1)

/-!

#### A.1.1. Extensionality lemma

We prove an extensionality lemma for `InitialConditions`.
That is, a lemma which states that two initial conditions are equal if their
initial positions and initial velocities are equal.

-/

@[ext]
lemma InitialConditions.ext {IC₁ IC₂ : InitialConditions} (h1 : IC₁.x₀ = IC₂.x₀)
    (h2 : IC₁.v₀ = IC₂.v₀) : IC₁ = IC₂ := by
  cases IC₁
  cases IC₂
  simp_all

/-!

### A.2. Relation to other types of initial conditions

We relate the initial condition given by an initial position and an initial velocity
to other specifications of initial conditions. This is currently not implemented,
and is a TODO.

-/

TODO "6VZME" "Implement other initial conditions. For example:
- initial conditions at a given time.
- Two positions at different times.
- Two velocities at different times.
And convert them into the type `InitialConditions` above (which may need generalzing a bit
to make this possible)."

/-!

### A.3. The zero initial conditions

The zero initial conditions are the initial conditions with zero initial position
and zero initial velocity.

In the end, we will see that this corresponds to the solution which is identically zero,
i.e. the particle remains at rest at the origin.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-- The zero initial condition. -/
instance : Zero InitialConditions := ⟨0, 0⟩

/-!

#### A.3.1. Simple results for the zero initial conditions

Some simple results about the zero initial conditions.

-/
/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting point. -/
@[simp]
lemma x₀_zero : x₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

/-- The zero initial condition has zero starting velocity. -/
@[simp]
lemma v₀_zero : v₀ 0 = 0 := rfl

end InitialConditions
/-!

## B. Trajectories associated with the initial conditions

To each initial condition we association a trajectory. We will prove some basic properties
of these trajectories.

Eventually we will show that these trajectories satisfy the equation of motion, for
now we can think of them as some choice of trajectory associated with the initial conditions.

-/

namespace InitialConditions

/-!

### B.1. The trajectory associated with the initial conditions

-/

/-- Given initial conditions, the solution to the classical harmonic oscillator. -/
noncomputable def trajectory (IC : InitialConditions) : Time → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 1) := fun t =>
  cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀

/-!

#### B.1.1. Definitional equality for the trajectory

We show a basic definitional equality for the trajectory.

-/
lemma trajectory_eq (IC : InitialConditions) :
    IC.trajectory S = fun t : Time => cos (S.ω * t) • IC.x₀ + (sin (S.ω * t)/S.ω) • IC.v₀ := rfl

/-!

### B.2. The trajectory for zero initial conditions

The trajectory for zero initial conditions is the zero function.

-/

/-- For zero initial conditions, the trajectory is zero. -/
@[simp]
lemma trajectory_zero : trajectory S 0 = fun _ => 0 := by
  simp [trajectory_eq]

/-!

### B.3. Smoothness of the trajectories

The trajectories for any initial conditions are smooth functions of time.

-/

@[fun_prop]
lemma trajectory_contDiff (S : HarmonicOscillator) (IC : InitialConditions) {n : WithTop ℕ∞} :
    ContDiff ℝ n (IC.trajectory S) := by
  rw [trajectory_eq]
  apply ContDiff.add
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => cos x) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp contDiff_cos ?_)
    fun_prop
  apply ContDiff.smul _ contDiff_const
  · have hx := contDiff_sin (n := n)
    change ContDiff ℝ _ (((fun x => sin x / S.ω) ∘ (fun y => S.ω * y))∘ Time.toRealCLM)
    refine ContDiff.comp_continuousLinearMap (ContDiff.comp ?_ ?_)
    · fun_prop
    · fun_prop

/-!

### B.4. Velocity of the trajectories

We give a simplification of the velocity of the trajectory.

-/

lemma trajectory_velocity (IC : InitialConditions) : ∂ₜ (IC.trajectory S) =
    fun t : Time => - S.ω • sin (S.ω * t.val) • IC.x₀ + cos (S.ω * t.val) • IC.v₀ := by
  funext t
  rw [trajectory_eq, Time.deriv, fderiv_fun_add (by fun_prop) (by fun_prop)]
  rw [fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop), fderiv_smul_const (by fun_prop)]
  have h1 : (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val) / S.ω) t) =
    (1/ S.ω) • (fderiv ℝ (fun t => sin (S.ω * t.val)) t) := by
    rw [← fderiv_mul_const]
    congr
    funext t
    field_simp
    fun_prop
  simp [h1]
  rw [fderiv_cos (by fun_prop), fderiv_sin (by fun_prop),
    fderiv_fun_mul

intro h
  rcases h with ⟨heq, hx0, hderiv0⟩
```

We'll show that `y(t)` satisfies the homogeneous equation `y'' + S.ω^2 y = 0`, and also `y(0) = 0` and `y'(0) = 0`. The only solution to this is `y(t) = 0` for all `t`.
